nativeRange.habitat: Rocky intertidal zones and shallow coral reef flats in high-energy, wave-swept environments. In the wild, the species tends to form dense colonies on exposed rock surfaces.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical coral reef intertidal and shallow subtidal

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish that may graze it heavily (tangs, surgeonfish, sea urchins)

imageCandidates.notes: Narrissa Spies; 4608x3456. Tack-sharp, vibrant yellow-brown specimen showing the diagnostic toothed, top-shaped (turbinate) leaflets on a central stalk with perfect clarity; strongest ID confidence. Slight caveat: held in hand, out of water.
Philippe Bourjon (Reunion); 1200x1365. In-water shot of a single upright thallus in reef habitat, showing the spiny turbinate leaflets; slight epiphyte fuzz but good in-situ context.
Philippe Bourjon (Reunion); 1200x858. In-situ whole erect plant growing on coral in blue water; complete plant framed against reef, adds habitat variety.

summary: The Ornate Turbanweed (Turbinaria ornata) is a rare Indo-Pacific brown alga with small, toothed, top-shaped leaflets arranged on a central stalk. Very slow-growing and seldom available in the hobby, it is generally considered best suited to experienced reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Ornate Turbanweed is a brown macroalga — belonging to the same family as Sargassum, within the order Fucales — and is also known in Hawaiian tradition as Limu Kahili. The species features small, toothed, turbinate (cone- or top-shaped) bladder-like leaflets arranged on a central stalk, typically ranging from yellow-brown to dark brown.

IMPORTANT TRADE-NAME DISAMBIGUATION: The genus name "Turbinaria" is shared by two completely unrelated organisms: Turbinaria ornata, a brown macroalga in order Fucales; and Turbinaria spp. (cup corals / scroll corals), which are stony corals in the family Dendrophylliidae, also known as disc coral, vase coral, or pagoda coral. These are entirely different life forms — one is a photosynthetic alga, the other is a colonial cnidarian animal. When searching for or purchasing either organism, confirming the full scientific name and the seller's category (alga vs. coral) is strongly advised.

Care requirements are reportedly similar to Sargassum, suggesting moderate lighting is adequate. The species is generally considered appropriate for seasoned aquarists, reflecting its slow growth and sensitivity compared to fast-growing refugium macroalgae.

In the wild, T. ornata is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific and central Pacific, where it can become locally dominant on rocky intertidal reef zones. It disperses by releasing buoyant older fronds that travel on surface currents. Pharmaceutical research has identified bioactive compounds with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antidiabetic properties in wild-collected material.

This species is very rarely available in the hobby trade and is frequently listed as sold out. Standard reef water parameters (SG 1.023–1.026, 23–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4) are appropriate. Propagation in captivity is by fragmentation of the thallus. As a brown alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand — it should be attached to rockwork using superglue, zip-tie, fishing line, or by wedging it into a crevice instead.
breedingNotes: In captivity, propagation is typically by fragmentation: detaching a portion and anchoring it to rockwork. Sexual reproduction involves alternation of gametophyte and sporophyte generations, as is typical in brown algae, but this has not been documented as a meaningful propagation path in hobby aquariums.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source. Confirmed common name Ornate Turbanweed, scientific name Turbinaria ornata, very slow growth rate, turbulent-water preference, intermediate/advanced care level, and compatibility with reef invertebrates.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Turbinaria ornata
additional.notes: Confirmed family Sargassaceae, order Fucales, class Phaeophyceae; native range Indo-Pacific/central Pacific; adult size 2–20 cm; intertidal reef habitat; Hawaiian name Limu Kahili. Used for taxonomy, native range, and size data.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Turbinaria (coral)
additional.notes: Consulted to document trade-name collision: Turbinaria corals are order Scleractinia, family Dendrophylliidae — entirely unrelated to the brown alga Turbinaria ornata.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
